source: The Anatomy of a Golf Course
author: Tom Doak

chunks.principle: Strategic design places fairway hazards only where they make the optimum line of attack risky, like pocket billiards — the expert seeks not only to advance the ball, but to position it for the best subsequent shot.
chunks.application: Think of each hole like a billiards problem. Your drive isn't just about avoiding hazards — it's about positioning for the approach. A drive to the 'wrong' side of the fairway, even in the fairway, can cost you as much as a drive in light rough on the right side.

chunks.principle: Difficult hazards are the essence of interesting golf, especially in strategic design, where there ought to be a distinct penalty for the golfer who has failed to heed the hazard after being given plenty of room to avoid it.
chunks.application: Deep bunkers and tough hazards are not 'unfair' — they exist because the architect gave you a wide alternate route. If you find yourself in a severe hazard, the lesson is about your course management, not the hazard's severity.

chunks.principle: The entrance to the green is the most overlooked part of modern design. There should almost always be an open approach through which a low, running shot can bounce onto the green. Architects design this for the average player.
chunks.application: Always look for the opening in front of the green. Even when bunkers guard the sides, the front entrance is often deliberately left clear. A low running approach through the entrance is safer than a high aerial shot that must carry hazards.

chunks.principle: The relationship that matters most is not green size versus approach length, but the size of the hazard-free area around the hole versus the difficulty of the approach. If properly balanced, the course remains fair for all players.
chunks.application: When a green looks tiny and intimidating, check around the pin position — there's usually a generous area where you can safely miss. Aim for the safe zone rather than firing at the flag. The architect made it fair if you play smart.

chunks.principle: Both strategic and heroic design require the golfer to weigh risks against advantages and think out their attack from the green backward, based on an honest assessment of their own abilities. Self-deception is the greatest enemy.
chunks.application: Before every tee shot, make an honest assessment: Can I actually carry that bunker, or am I flattering myself? The architect designed the hole to reward honest self-assessment and punish overconfidence.

chunks.principle: The simplest strategic holes produce the most interesting play. A clear hazard on one side with 100 shades of grey on the other forces the golfer to weigh how much risk they are willing to take against how much margin for error they need.
chunks.application: When you see trouble clearly on one side of a hole, don't just aim away from it — consider how close to it you dare play. Playing tighter to the hazard gives a better angle to the green. Find your personal comfort line.

chunks.principle: On dogleg holes, the architect must balance giving the long hitter a chance to cut the corner while ensuring the short hitter isn't blocked from a full second shot. The fairway width in the landing zone reveals the intended strategy.
chunks.application: On doglegs, measure whether your driver can safely reach the corner or if it risks going through the fairway. Sometimes a shorter club to the widest part of the fairway in the bend gives you a better angle than an aggressive cut.

chunks.principle: Par-3 holes have less strategy because all but the weakest players aim for the green. Their beauty comes from the picture and the hazards. John Low wrote: 'At the short hole bunkers should abound, so fearful that the player delivers his tee shot almost without hope of escape.'
chunks.application: On par 3s, commit fully to center of green. The hazards are designed to be visually intimidating. A confident swing to the middle beats a tentative swing at the flag every time. Leave the pin-hunting for days your swing is grooved.

chunks.principle: On between-length holes — driveable par-4s, long par-4s, and short par-5s — the strong player who goes for the green must show unusual skill as well as strength. The golfer who admits their weakness should be rewarded for discretion.
chunks.application: On reachable par 5s, honestly assess whether your second shot can hold the green. If bunkers or trouble guard the green, laying up to your favorite wedge distance is often the smarter play. Discretion often beats aggression here.

chunks.principle: Fairway width has enormous impact on playability. Fairways less than 30 yards wide are U.S. Open standards — far too narrow for most recreational golfers. The best holes feature 50-60 yard wide fairways that still create strategic interest.
chunks.application: If a fairway looks tight, it might simply be an illusion of mowing patterns or shadows. Many seemingly narrow holes actually have playable rough on one side. Focus on keeping the ball in the short grass area, not just the 'official' fairway.

chunks.principle: Wind can make short shots more difficult than long ones. On links courses in a gale, the standard high shot to a par-3 is nearly impossible to judge — the golfer must produce a low, punched shot, a difficult prospect to a small target.
chunks.application: In strong wind, use a lower-lofted club and a shorter swing rather than fighting the wind with your normal shot. A 6-iron played lower in a headwind is far more predictable than an 8-iron trying to hold its line.

chunks.principle: Green speed is not the same as putting difficulty. Proper difficulty is a combination of slope and speed. Many clubs maintain their greens too fast for the contours the architect designed, making certain pin positions nearly impossible.
chunks.application: When greens are fast, give extra care to leaving yourself uphill putts — not because downhill putts are much harder statistically, but because misjudging speed downhill can turn a 6-footer into a 12-footer coming back.

chunks.principle: An architect hates to explain their holes. Sometimes even the designer cannot figure out the best method of attack until playing the hole several times. When you can't read the strategy immediately, that's a sign of interesting design.
chunks.application: Don't get frustrated if a hole 'doesn't make sense' on first play. Great holes reveal their secrets over multiple rounds. Take notes on what worked and what didn't — your strategy for that hole will sharpen over time.

chunks.principle: By leaving one side of the green undefended, architects induce golfers to miss toward safety — even though the open side may be harder to get up and down from due to green contours. The green without bunkers also hinders depth perception.
chunks.application: The 'safe' side of the green isn't always easy. When one side is conspicuously free of bunkers, there's often a tricky slope or tight lie waiting there. The center of the green is still your best target.

chunks.principle: Forward tees are not miracle makers. The real solution to making a course fair for all levels is ensuring every player has a chance to play all the shots of the game — including a running approach, not just forced aerial carries.
chunks.application: Choose the right tees for your game so every hole allows you to make strategic decisions, not just survival choices. If you can't reach the fairway with your driver on multiple holes, you're playing from the wrong tees.
